docs.title: MK APEX Anim Picker — User Guide
docs.text: A fast, artist-friendly animation picker for Houdini APEX rigs. Build custom button layouts, assign controls, capture poses, and run scripts — all from a single panel.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text: 01 — Getting Started Install, open the panel, interface overview 02 — Select Mode Select controls, shift/ctrl/alt+click, bulk assign 03 — Pose Mode Capture, apply, and blend poses 04 — Script Mode Run Python scripts from buttons 04b — Script API Reference Full injected variable and helper reference 05 — Edit Mode Create and arrange buttons 06 — Button Properties Color, shape, label, mode, controls 07 — Import Layout Import from APEX rig or SVG file 08 — Canvas & Background Background image, grid, snap, lock 09 — Saving & Loading Save/load picker files, auto-save, path conflicts 10 — Zones Create and organize zones on the canvas 11 — Shortcuts Full keyboard and mouse reference

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: • Houdini 21.0+

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Unzip the MKAnimPicker folder
  2. Copy it into your Houdini preferences directory or in a directory of your choice: 
    $HOUDINI_USER_PREF_DIR/   (e.g. Documents/houdini21.0/)
  3. Open the MK_AnimPicker.json package file and verify the paths match your installation location. If you placed the folder somewhere else, update the paths accordingly.
  4. Restart Houdini.

docs.title: Opening the Panel
docs.text:
Create a new pane tab and search for MK_APEX_Anim_Picker in the panel list.

The panel can be docked anywhere in your Houdini layout like any other pane.

docs.title: Interface Overview
docs.text: Area Description Edit/File bar Toolbar row with zone controls, edit mode toggle, save/load tab actions. Tab bar Switch between picker layouts. Each tab is independent. Canvas The button layout area. Scroll to zoom, middle-mouse drag to pan. Rig menu Dropdown to select which rig the picker operates on. Lock button Padlock icon. Prevents accidental edits in select mode. Zone HUD Row of colored squares — one per zone. Visible when zones exist.

docs.title: Tabs
docs.text: Create a tab — click the + button in the tab bar. Rename a tab — double-click the tab name, or use the Rename field in the edit panel. Delete a tab — right-click the tab → Delete Tab. Reorder tabs — drag and drop.

docs.title: Lock Mode
docs.text:
The padlock icon in the top-right corner toggles lock mode.

When locked, entering edit mode and reassigning controls is disabled. Selection, pose, and script buttons all work normally.

Useful when animating — prevents accidental layout changes.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
A first picker from scratch in under a minute.

  1. Open the panel — create a new pane tab, search for MK_APEX_Anim_Picker
  2. Rename it — double-click the tab name, type a name, press Enter
  3. Enter Edit Mode — click the pencil icon top-right
  4. Add a button — right-click the canvas and choose Edit Controls
  5. Assign a controller — in the APEX viewport select a control, click Add Selected in the popup, then close it
  6. Exit Edit Mode — click the pencil icon again
  7. Test it — click the button, the control gets selected in the viewport
  8. Save — File → Save Tab, choose a location for your .json file

That's the core loop. From here see Edit Mode to build out a full layout, or Pose Mode to start capturing poses.

Select Mode →

docs.title: Select Mode
docs.text: Select mode buttons select APEX controls in the viewport when clicked. This is the default button mode.

docs.title: Clicking Buttons
docs.text:
Action Result Click Select the assigned controls (clears previous selection) Shift+click Add assigned controls to the current selection Ctrl+click Deselect this button only Alt+click Select both the assigned controls and their mirrored counterparts

Mirror matching uses the rig's built-in mirror pattern — the picker swaps left/right counterparts automatically.

docs.title: Assigning Controls to a Button
docs.text:
Controls are assigned per button via the right-click context menu.

Option Result Edit Controls Opens the Selection Manager dialog — view, add, or remove controls individually Reassign to Selection Replaces all assigned controls with the current viewport selection Add Selection to Button Appends the current viewport selection to the existing controls

docs.title: Selection Manager
docs.text:
For precise control over what's assigned to a button, right-click → Edit Controls.

The Selection Manager dialog lets you: - View all assigned control paths - Add controls from the current viewport selection - Remove individual controls

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• A button with no controls assigned does nothing when clicked.
  • Control paths are full APEX paths, e.g. /char/Base.rig/L_arm_ctrl.
  • If the rig is changed in the rig menu, the picker remaps controls to the new rig automatically where controller names match.

← Getting Started · Pose Mode →

docs.title: Pose Mode
docs.text: Pose mode buttons store a snapshot of control values at a specific frame. Clicking the button applies that pose back to the rig.

docs.title: Capturing a Pose
docs.text:
1. In the APEX viewport, select the controls you want to include in the pose
  2. Set the rig to the desired pose
  3. In the picker, right-click the button → Capture Pose

The button stores the pose and displays a thumbnail of the viewport at capture time.

docs.title: Applying a Pose
docs.text:
Click the button — the stored pose is applied to the rig at the current frame.

The picker remaps the pose to the rig selected in the rig menu, so one pose button can be used across multiple characters if their controller names match.

docs.title: Blend Strip
docs.text:
Each pose button has a blend strip along its bottom edge.

Drag horizontally across the strip to blend between the current rig pose and the stored pose.

Position Result Left (0%) Current rig pose unchanged Right (100%) Full stored pose applied

The blend is live — the rig updates as you drag. Releasing commits the blended value.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Pose data is stored in the picker's JSON file — that is the only file you need to save and share.

← Select Mode · Script Mode →

docs.title: Script Mode
docs.text: Script mode buttons run Python code when clicked. Useful for IK/FK switching, toggling rig options, selecting control sets, or any custom automation.

docs.title: Setting Up a Script Button
docs.text:
1. Right-click a button → Script (works in any mode)
  2. Right-click the button → Edit Script
  3. Write your code in the editor and click Save

The editor supports Python syntax highlighting, line numbers, and 4-space indentation.

docs.title: Injected Variables
docs.text:
Available automatically — no import needed:

Variable Type Description mk_rig_path str Full APEX rig path of the selected rig. Empty if set to None. mk_rig_name str Short rig name (first path segment). mk_tab_name str Name of the tab the button lives on.

docs.title: Injected Functions
docs.text:
Function Description mk_get_parm / mk_set_parm / mk_toggle_parm Read, set, or toggle a rig graph parm. mk_space_switch Match-and-switch (IK/FK snap). mk_show_ctrls / mk_hide_ctrls / mk_toggle_visibility Show, hide, or toggle control visibility. mk_select_ctrls Select controls in the viewport. mk_btn_disable / mk_btn_enable / mk_btn_hide / mk_btn_show Control picker button state by tag. mk_sync_ctrls Force control manager to flush pending visibility changes. mk_state Raw APEX animate state — for advanced use.

All functions operate on the current rig automatically. See Script API Reference for full signatures and examples.

docs.title: IK/FK Toggle
docs.text:
import apex.ui.statecommandutils as scu

if not mk_rig_path:
    print("No rig selected")
else:
    rig = scu.getSceneData(mk_rig_path)
    parms = rig.graph_parms
    current = parms.get("L_arm_ikfk_x", 0.0)
    parms.set("L_arm_ikfk_x", 0.0 if current >= 0.5 else 1.0)

docs.title: Select All Controls on Current Rig
docs.text:
import apex.ui.statecommandutils as scu

if mk_rig_path:
    ctrl_mgr = scu.getSceneControlManager()
    all_ctrls = [p for p in ctrl_mgr.controls if p.startswith(mk_rig_path)]
    scu.sendCommand("selectControls", {
        "controls": all_ctrls,
        "controls_in_tree_format": False,
    })

docs.title: Tab-Specific Logic
docs.text:
if mk_tab_name == "Body":
    print("Body tab")
elif mk_tab_name == "Face":
    print("Face tab")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Always check mk_rig_path before using it — it is empty when the rig menu is set to None.
  • Errors print to the Houdini Python console. Add print() statements to debug.
  • Use import normally for anything else (hou, apex, etc.).

For the full helper and API reference see Script API Reference.

← Pose Mode · Script API Reference →

docs.title: Script API Reference
docs.text: Full reference for script mode buttons. For an overview see 04 — Script Mode.

docs.title: Setting up a Script Button
docs.text:
1. Right-click a button → Script (works in any mode)
  2. Right-click the button → Edit Script
  3. Write your Python code and click Save

docs.title: Injected Variables
docs.text:
Available automatically when the script runs — no imports needed:

Variable Type Example value Description mk_rig_path str /electra_wood.char/Base.rig Full APEX rig path of the rig currently selected in the picker menu. Empty string if set to None. mk_rig_name str electra_wood.char First path segment of the rig — useful as a short identifier. mk_tab_name str Body Name of the tab the button lives on.

docs.title: Injected Functions
docs.text:
Also available automatically — all operate on the current rig:

Function Description mk_get_parm(parm_name) Read a graph parm value from the current rig. mk_set_parm(parm_name, value, ctrl="") Set a graph parm value and update the viewport. mk_toggle_parm(parm_name, ctrl="") Toggle a graph parm between two float values. mk_space_switch(ctrl_name, set_keys=False) Trigger a match-and-switch (IK/FK snap) on a space-switch control. mk_show_ctrls(ctrl_names) Show a list of controls by short name. mk_hide_ctrls(ctrl_names) Hide a list of controls by short name. mk_toggle_visibility(ctrl_names) Toggle visibility — any visible → hide all, all hidden → show all. mk_select_ctrls(ctrl_names) Select controls in the viewport by short name. Accepts a string or list. mk_btn_disable(tags) Gray out picker buttons that have the given tag(s). They become non-clickable. mk_btn_enable(tags) Re-enable picker buttons that have the given tag(s). mk_btn_hide(tags) Hide picker buttons that have the given tag(s). mk_btn_show(tags) Show picker buttons that have the given tag(s). mk_sync_ctrls() Force the control manager to process pending visibility changes. Call after show/hide if controls don't update immediately. mk_state Raw APEX animate state object. For advanced use when the helpers aren't enough.

docs.title: Finding the Right Parm and Ctrl Names
docs.text: Before writing a toggle button you need two things: 1. The parm name — the graph parm that stores the value (e.g. R_arm_ikfk_x) 2. The ctrl name — the abstract controller that drives it (e.g. R_arm_config_ikfkblend)

docs.title: Step 1 — Print all arm parms
docs.text:
Run this in a script button (or the Houdini Python Shell) to see all parms for a limb:

import apex.ui.statecommandutils as scu

rig = scu.getSceneData(mk_rig_path)
parms = rig.graph_parms
for k, v in sorted(parms.items()):
    if "R_arm" in k:
        print(k, "=", v)

On Electra this prints (among others): 

R_arm_config_ikfkblend = 0.0
R_arm_config_ikfk_switch = False
R_arm_ikfk_x = 0.0

  • R_arm_ikfk_x — the blend float (0 = IK, 1 = FK). Use mk_toggle_parm.
  • R_arm_config_ikfk_switch — the match-and-switch trigger. Use mk_space_switch.

docs.title: Step 2 — Find the ctrl name for a float parm
docs.text:
The ctrl is the short name of the abstract controller that owns the parm. To find it, click the IK/FK slider in the APEX viewport and check the Selected controls list — the controller whose name contains ikfkblend is the one.

You can also print the control map:

import apex.ui.statecommandutils as scu

state_args = {}
scu.sendCommand("getState", state_args)
state = state_args.get("state")
ctrl_mgr = state.control_manager

rig_path = mk_rig_path
for path, ctrl in ctrl_mgr.controls.items():
    if "R_arm_config" in path:
        print(path)

On Electra the relevant controllers are: 

/electra.char/Base.rig/R_arm_config_ikfkblend   ← drives R_arm_ikfk_x
/electra.char/Base.rig/R_arm_config_ikfk_switch ← match-and-switch trigger

The short name (without rig path prefix) is what you pass as ctrl.

docs.title: Example: IK/FK Toggle with Linked Visibility
docs.text:
Toggles the blend and swaps which controls are visible. Replace the parm/ctrl names and control lists with the ones from your character:

fk = ["L_Hand_fk", "L_Lowerarm_Twist_01_fk", "L_Upperarm_fk", "L_Upperarm_Twist_01_fk", "L_Lowerarm_fk"]
ik = ["L_armTarget_ik", "L_armPolevec_ik", "L_armRoot_ik"]

ikfk_parm_name = "L_arm_blendikfkblend_x"

mk_toggle_parm(ikfk_parm_name, ctrl="L_arm_blendikfkblend_abst")

if mk_get_parm(ikfk_parm_name) >= 0.5:  # now FK
    mk_show_ctrls(fk)
    mk_hide_ctrls(ik)
else:  # now IK
    mk_show_ctrls(ik)
    mk_hide_ctrls(fk)

docs.title: Example: IK/FK Blend Toggle (Electra)
docs.text:
Toggles the arm between IK and FK smoothly (0 ↔ 1):

mk_toggle_parm("R_arm_ikfk_x", ctrl="R_arm_config_ikfkblend")

docs.title: Example: IK/FK Match-and-Switch (Electra)
docs.text:
Snaps the IK or FK controls to match the current pose, then switches:

mk_space_switch("R_arm_config_ikfk_switch")

Pass set_keys=True to also key the controls at the current frame:

mk_space_switch("R_arm_config_ikfk_switch", set_keys=True)

docs.title: mk_get_parm(parm_name, default=0.0)
docs.text:
Returns the current value of a graph parm on the active rig.

val = mk_get_parm("R_arm_ikfk_x")

docs.title: mk_set_parm(parm_name, value, ctrl="")
docs.text:
Sets a graph parm and pushes the update to the viewport and channel editor.

mk_set_parm("R_arm_ikfk_x", 1.0, ctrl="R_arm_config_ikfkblend")

docs.title: mk_toggle_parm(parm_name, ctrl="", on=1.0, off=0.0, threshold=0.5)
docs.text:
Reads the current value and flips between on and off.

mk_toggle_parm("R_arm_ikfk_x", ctrl="R_arm_config_ikfkblend")

The ctrl argument is the short name of the abstract controller that drives the parm. It is needed so the channel binding updates correctly and the value doesn't snap back.

docs.title: mk_space_switch(ctrl_name, set_keys=False)
docs.text:
Triggers a match-and-switch on a space-switch control — snaps the rig pose then switches IK/FK (or any space). set_keys=True also keys the controls at the current frame.

mk_space_switch("R_arm_config_ikfk_switch")

All helpers operate on mk_rig_path automatically — no need to pass the rig path.

docs.title: mk_select_ctrls(ctrl_names)
docs.text:
Selects one or more controls in the APEX viewport. Accepts a single string or a list.

mk_select_ctrls("R_Hand_ctrl")
mk_select_ctrls(["R_Hand_ctrl", "R_armTarget_ik"])

docs.title: mk_show_ctrls(ctrl_names) / mk_hide_ctrls(ctrl_names)
docs.text:
Show or hide controls by their short name (without the rig path prefix).

mk_show_ctrls(["L_Hand_fk", "L_Upperarm_fk"])
mk_hide_ctrls("L_armTarget_ik")

docs.title: mk_toggle_visibility(ctrl_names)
docs.text:
If any of the listed controls are visible, hides all. If all are hidden, shows all.

mk_toggle_visibility(["Eye_Look_Static_Ctrl", "Eye_Look_Follow_Ctrl"])

docs.title: Button Tag API
docs.text: Tags are user-defined strings assigned to picker buttons in the edit panel. Script buttons can use them to gray out or hide groups of buttons at runtime.

docs.title: mk_btn_disable(tags) / mk_btn_enable(tags)
docs.text:
Gray out (disable) or re-enable buttons by tag. Disabled buttons are non-clickable and shown at reduced opacity.

mk_btn_disable("ik_arm")        # single tag
mk_btn_disable(["ik_arm", "ik_leg"])  # multiple tags
mk_btn_enable("fk_arm")

docs.title: mk_btn_hide(tags) / mk_btn_show(tags)
docs.text:
Hide or show picker buttons by tag.

mk_btn_hide("fk_arm")
mk_btn_show("ik_arm")

docs.title: Example: IK/FK Button State Toggle
docs.text:
Assign tags "ik_arm" and "fk_arm" to the relevant buttons in the edit panel, then use a script button:

if mk_get_parm("L_arm_blendikfkblend_x") >= 0.5:  # currently FK
    mk_btn_enable("fk_arm")
    mk_btn_disable("ik_arm")
else:  # currently IK
    mk_btn_enable("ik_arm")
    mk_btn_disable("fk_arm")

Tags and the disabled/hidden state are saved to the picker JSON file.

docs.title: Raw snippet (no helpers)
docs.text:
If you need full control:

import apex.ui.statecommandutils as scu
import hou

if not mk_rig_path:
    print("No rig selected in picker")
else:
    state_args = {}
    scu.sendCommand("getState", state_args)
    state = state_args.get("state")

    rig = scu.getSceneData(mk_rig_path)
    parms = rig.graph_parms
    current = parms.get("R_arm_ikfk_x", 0.0)
    parms.set("R_arm_ikfk_x", 0.0 if current >= 0.5 else 1.0)

    if state:
        original = list(state.control_manager.controlPaths())
        state._selectControls([f"{mk_rig_path}/R_arm_config_ikfkblend"])
        state._updatePendingFromBindings()
        state.scene.updateEvaluationParms(hou.frame())
        state.control_manager.update(state.scene)
        state.updateChannelPrimsFromParmComponents(components=["x"])
        state.pushChannels()
        state.scene_viewer.curViewport().draw()
        state._selectControls(original)

docs.title: Example: Select All Controls on Current Rig
docs.text:
import apex.ui.statecommandutils as scu

if mk_rig_path:
    ctrl_mgr = scu.getSceneControlManager()
    all_ctrls = [p for p in ctrl_mgr.controls.keys() if p.startswith(mk_rig_path)]
    scu.sendCommand("selectControls", {
        "controls": all_ctrls,
        "controls_in_tree_format": False,
    })

docs.title: Example: Tab-Specific Logic
docs.text:
if mk_tab_name == "Body":
    print("On Body tab")
elif mk_tab_name == "Face":
    print("On Face tab")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• mk_rig_path is empty when the rig menu is set to None. All helpers will print a warning and do nothing if no rig is selected.
  • The script runs in a restricted namespace — only the injected variables and standard Python/Houdini imports are available. Use import normally for anything else (hou, apex, scu, etc.).
  • rig.graph_parms is a live APEX dict — .set() changes are immediate.

← Script Mode · Edit Mode →

docs.title: Edit Mode
docs.text: Edit mode lets you build and arrange the picker layout. Toggle it with the pencil icon in the top-right corner of the panel.

docs.title: Adding Buttons
docs.text:
Three ways to add buttons:

Method How Add Button Edit panel → Buttons section → Add Button. Places a new button at the center of the canvas. Import from APEX Layout Reads control positions from the rig and creates a button for each one, arranged spatially. Import SVG Layout Imports an SVG file and creates buttons from its shapes. Right-click canvas Right-click empty canvas area → Add Button

docs.title: Selecting Buttons
docs.text: Action Result Click a button Select it Shift+click Add to selection Drag on empty canvas Rubber-band select Ctrl+A Select all

docs.title: Moving Buttons
docs.text: Drag a selected button to move it. Hold Shift while dragging to move multiple selected buttons together.

docs.title: Resizing Buttons
docs.text:
Drag the triangle handle at the bottom-right corner of a button.

Hold Shift while dragging to resize proportionally. Enable Lock W=H in the edit panel to always resize proportionally.

docs.title: Deleting Buttons
docs.text: Select buttons and press Delete or Backspace.

docs.title: Copy / Paste
docs.text:
Action Shortcut Copy selected buttons Ctrl+C Paste Ctrl+V

Pasted buttons are offset slightly from the originals. All properties are preserved — controls, mode, script, pose data, shape, color, flip.

docs.title: Undo / Redo
docs.text:
Use the Undo and Redo buttons in the edit toolbar. Only available in edit mode.

Covers: add, delete, move, resize, color, shape, flip, rename.

docs.title: Align & Distribute
docs.text:
With multiple buttons selected, use the alignment tools in the edit panel:

  • Align left / center / right / top / middle / bottom
  • Distribute horizontally / vertically

← Script API Reference · Button Properties →

docs.title: Button Properties
docs.text: Select one or more buttons in Edit Mode to edit their properties in the right-side edit panel.

docs.title: Label
docs.text:
Type a new name in the Rename field and press Enter.

Use Search & Replace to rename multiple buttons at once — supports match case and whole word options.

docs.title: Color
docs.text: Click the color swatch to open the preset palette. Click the pipette icon to open the HSV color picker for a custom color.

docs.title: Shape
docs.text:
Shape Description Rect Rectangle (default) Circle Ellipse SVG Custom shape imported from an SVG icon file

Use Flip H / Flip V to mirror the shape horizontally or vertically.

docs.title: Mode
docs.text:
Sets what the button does when clicked in select mode:

Mode Description Select Selects APEX controls Pose Applies a stored pose Script Runs a Python script

docs.title: Size
docs.text: Use the W and H spinboxes to set an exact pixel size. Enable Lock W=H to constrain proportions.

docs.title: Controls (Select Mode)
docs.text:
Shows the APEX control paths assigned to the button.

Right-click the button → Edit Controls to open the Selection Manager dialog. See Select Mode for all assignment options.

docs.title: Script (Script Mode)
docs.text:
Right-click the button → Edit Script to open the code editor.

See Script Mode for full details.

docs.title: Pose (Pose Mode)
docs.text:
Right-click the button → Capture Pose to store the current rig pose.

See Pose Mode for full details.

← Edit Mode · Import Layout →

docs.title: Import Layout
docs.text: Two ways to automatically generate a button layout instead of placing buttons manually.

docs.title: Import from APEX Layout
docs.text: Creates one button per control in the rig, positioned to match the control's location in 3D space projected onto a 2D plane.

docs.title: How to use
docs.text:
1. In the APEX viewport, select the rig controls you want to import
  2. In the edit panel → Buttons section, choose a plane: XY, XZ, or YZ
  3. Click Import

Buttons are created and placed on the canvas tryin to match the spatial layout of the rig.

docs.title: Plane selection
docs.text: Plane Use when XY Character faces the camera (front view) XZ Top-down layout (e.g. foot controls) YZ Side view layout (e.g hand fingers controls)

docs.title: After importing
docs.text:
• Each button is assigned its corresponding control automatically
  • Labels are set to the control's short name
  • Resize, recolor, and reorganize as needed

docs.title: Import SVG Layout
docs.text: Creates buttons from shapes in an SVG file. Useful for building a picker from a character silhouette or custom template.

docs.title: How to use
docs.text:
1. Edit panel → Buttons section → Import SVG Layout
  2. Select an SVG file

Each shape in the SVG becomes a button. Position, size, color, and shape are read from the SVG.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Complex paths are supported — the button preserves the SVG path data as its shape
  • Grouped shapes are flattened

docs.title: Import SVG Icon
docs.text:
To use a custom SVG as the shape of a single button (not a layout):

Edit panel → Shape section → Import SVG Icon

This sets the visual shape of the selected button to the SVG path. See Button Properties.

← Button Properties · Canvas & Background →

docs.title: Background Image
docs.text: A background image helps orient the picker layout — typically a character silhouette or body diagram.

docs.title: Loading a background
docs.text:
Edit panel → Background section → Load

Supported formats: PNG, JPG.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text:
Control Description Load Pick an image file Clear Remove the current background Opacity Slider — how transparent the image appears (0–100%) Scale Slider — scale the image relative to the canvas

The background image is embedded in the picker's JSON file — it travels with the layout.

If the image doesn't show up after loading, you may have saved with a $HIP path and opened from a different project.

docs.title: Grid
docs.text:
Edit panel → Grid section.

Option Description Show Grid Toggle grid visibility on the canvas Snap to Grid Snaps button positions to grid intersections when moving

docs.title: Canvas Navigation
docs.text:
Action How Pan Middle-mouse drag Zoom Scroll wheel Fit all buttons Press H Fit selected buttons Press G

← Import Layout · Saving & Loading →

docs.title: Saving
docs.text:
Open the File menu in the top bar.

Action Description Save Tab Save the current tab to a .json file Save All Tabs Save each tab individually — prompts for a path per tab Auto-save on Hip Save Toggle — when enabled, all tabs with a saved path are written automatically every time the Houdini .hip file is saved

The picker saves to a .json file. Everything is stored in that file: - Button labels, colors, shapes, modes, control assignments - Scripts - Pose data (including the full pose geometry for cross-session restore) - Background images (embedded as base64) - Zone layout

docs.title: Path Conflicts
docs.text: When Auto-save on Hip Save is enabled, if two or more tabs point to the same file the conflicting tabs are skipped and a warning is shown. Tabs with unique paths still save normally.

docs.title: Loading
docs.text: File → Load Tab(s) — opens a file picker. Multiple files can be selected at once; each becomes its own tab.

docs.title: File Location
docs.text:
The picker file can live anywhere. A good convention:

$HIP/picker/my_character.json

$HIP and other Houdini variables are supported in paths.

docs.title: Poses Across Sessions
docs.text: Pose data is fully self-contained in the JSON. Pose buttons work as soon as you reopen the file — no recapture needed.

docs.title: Multiple Characters
docs.text:
Each character or shot can have its own .json file. Load multiple files to get multiple tabs.

← Canvas & Background · Zones →

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: Input Action Scroll Wheel Zoom in / out Middle Click + Drag Pan H Fit all buttons in view G Fit selected buttons in view (falls back to fit-all if nothing selected)

docs.title: Selection (non-edit mode)
docs.text: Input Action Left Click (button) Select only that button, deselect all others Shift + Left Click (button) Add button to selection Ctrl + Left Click (button) Deselect that button Alt + Left Click (button) Select button + its mirror controls in APEX Left Drag (empty space) Replace selection — rubber-band (blue) Shift + Left Drag (empty space) Add to selection — rubber-band (orange) Ctrl + Left Drag (empty space) Remove from selection — rubber-band (red) Left Click (empty space) Deselect all (picker + APEX viewport)

docs.title: Edit Mode
docs.text: Input Action Left Click (button) Select only that button Shift + Left Click (button) Add to selection Ctrl + Left Click (button) Deselect that button Left Drag (button) Move selected buttons Left Drag (resize handle) Resize button Double Click (button) Rename button Right Click (button) Context menu Delete / Backspace Delete selected buttons Ctrl + C Copy selected buttons Ctrl + V Paste buttons

docs.title: Zone Edit Mode
docs.text:
Input Action Shift + Left Drag (empty canvas) Draw a new zone Left Drag (zone) Move zone Left Drag (zone edge/corner) Resize zone Left Click (zone) Select zone Delete / Backspace Delete selected zone Left Drag (zone square in HUD) Reorder zones Left Click (zone square in HUD) Zoom canvas to that zone / select zone in zone edit mode

← Zones

docs.title: Zones
docs.text: Zones are colored regions drawn behind the buttons to visually group and label areas of the picker canvas. Up to 12 zones per tab.

docs.title: Entering Zone Edit Mode
docs.text:
Right-click the canvas → Edit Zones, or use the context menu when no edit mode is active.

The zone edit panel appears on the right. Button interaction is disabled while zone edit is active. The lock button hides while in zone edit mode, same as in button edit mode.

Exit zone edit the same way — right-click → Exit Zone Edit.

docs.title: Creating a Zone
docs.text:
Shift + drag on empty canvas — draw a rectangle to create a new zone. The zone is assigned a color automatically from the preset palette.

Alternatively, use Add Zone in the zone edit panel to place one at the canvas center.

docs.title: Selecting a Zone
docs.text: Click a zone to select it, or click its colored square in the Zone HUD. The zone edit panel updates to show its name and color.

docs.title: Renaming a Zone
docs.text: With a zone selected, type a new name in the Name field of the zone edit panel and press Enter.

docs.title: Recoloring a Zone
docs.text: With a zone selected, click the color swatch in the zone edit panel to pick a new color.

docs.title: Moving and Resizing a Zone
docs.text: Drag the zone body to move it. Drag a zone edge or corner to resize it.

docs.title: Deleting a Zone
docs.text: Select a zone and press Delete or Backspace, or click Delete Zone in the zone edit panel.

docs.title: Zone HUD
docs.text:
When zones exist, a row of colored squares appears in the top bar — one square per zone.

Action Result Click a square Zoom canvas to that zone (normal mode) / select zone (zone edit mode) Drag a square left/right (zone edit mode) Reorder zones

Zones that don't fit in the available space overflow into a ••• menu.
